Page 3

Warmup Example — Classifying an ODE

\[\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{x + y}{x}.\]

We check which type(s) this ODE belongs to:

Is this separable? No — it cannot be written as a product of a function of \(x\) alone and a function of \(y\) alone.

Is this linear? Yes. Rewriting:

\[\frac{dy}{dx} = 1 + \frac{1}{x}\,y \implies \frac{dy}{dx} - \frac{1}{x}\,y = 1. \checkmark\]

Is this exact? No.

Is this homogeneous? Yes, since substituting \(ax\) and \(ay\) for \(x\) and \(y\) gives

\[\frac{ax + ay}{ax} = \frac{x + y}{x},\]

the same function.

Page 4

Solution via Homogeneous Substitution

Dividing numerator and denominator by \(x\):

\[\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{x + y}{x} = \frac{1 + \frac{y}{x}}{1}.\]

This is a function of \(\frac{y}{x}\) alone, confirming the ODE is homogeneous. Let \(u = \dfrac{y}{x}\), so \(y = ux\) and by the product rule \(\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{du}{dx}\cdot x + u\). Substituting:

\[\frac{du}{dx}\cdot x + u = 1 + u.\]

The \(u\) terms cancel, leaving:

\[\frac{du}{dx} = \frac{1}{x}.\]

Integrating both sides:

\[u = \ln x + C.\]

Substituting back \(u = \dfrac{y}{x}\):

General Solution:

\[y = ux = x\ln x + Cx.\]

Page 5

Verification via Linear Method

As a check, we solve the same ODE using the linear (integrating factor) technique. The standard form is:

\[\frac{dy}{dx} - \frac{1}{x}\,y = 1, \quad P(x) = -\frac{1}{x},\quad Q(x) = 1.\]

The integrating factor is:

\[\mu(x) = e^{\int -\frac{1}{x}\,dx} = e^{-\ln x} = x^{-1} = \frac{1}{x}.\]

Multiplying through by \(\mu(x)\):

\[\frac{d}{dx}\!\left[y \cdot \frac{1}{x}\right] = \frac{1}{x}.\]

Integrating both sides:

\[y \cdot \frac{1}{x} = \ln x + C.\]

General Solution (same as before):

\[y = x\ln x + Cx. \checkmark\]

Page 6

Homogeneous ODEs — Summary

Definition: The ODE \(\dfrac{dy}{dx} = f(x,y)\) is homogeneous if it can be written as

\[\frac{dy}{dx} = f\!\left(\frac{y}{x}\right),\]

i.e., if substituting \(ax\) and \(ay\) for \(x\) and \(y\) yields the same function.

The substitution \(u = \dfrac{y}{x}\) (so \(y = ux\) and \(\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{du}{dx}\cdot x + u\)) transforms the ODE into:

\[\frac{du}{dx}\cdot x + u = f(u).\]

This is separable in \(u\) and \(x\).

Page 7

Example — Homogeneous ODE

\[\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{3x + y}{x - y}.\]

Divide numerator and denominator by \(x\):

\[\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{3 + \frac{y}{x}}{1 - \frac{y}{x}}.\]

This is a function of \(\dfrac{y}{x}\) alone. Check homogeneous. ✓

Substitute \(u = \dfrac{y}{x}\), so \(ux = y\) and \(\dfrac{du}{dx}\cdot x + u = \dfrac{dy}{dx}\):

\[\frac{du}{dx}\cdot x + u = \frac{3 + u}{1 - u}.\]

Page 8

Algebraic Simplification

Isolating \(\dfrac{du}{dx}\):

\[\frac{du}{dx}\cdot x = \frac{3+u}{1-u} - \frac{u(1-u)}{1-u} = \frac{3 + u - u + u^2}{1-u} = \frac{3 + u^2}{1 - u}.\]

Separating variables:

\[\frac{1-u}{3 + u^2}\,du = \frac{1}{x}\,dx.\]

This is separable and can in principle be integrated on both sides. The left-hand side integral is doable but not elementary — it requires partial fractions and an arctangent, but the process terminates.

Page 9

Example — Bernoulli Equation

\[\frac{dy}{dx} + \frac{1}{x}\,y = x\,y^2.\]

This is linear except for the \(y^2\) term on the right — it is a Bernoulli equation with \(n = 2\).

Divide through by \(y^2\):

\[y^{-2}\frac{dy}{dx} + \frac{1}{x}\,y^{-1} = x.\]

The term \(y^{-1}\) needs to become the new variable so the equation is linear.

Page 10

Bernoulli Substitution

Let \(u = y^{1-2} = y^{-1}\). Then by the chain rule:

\[\frac{du}{dx} = -1 \cdot y^{-2}\,\frac{dy}{dx}, \quad \text{i.e.,} \quad -\frac{du}{dx} = y^{-2}\frac{dy}{dx}.\]

Substituting \(-\dfrac{du}{dx}\) for \(y^{-2}\dfrac{dy}{dx}\) and \(u\) for \(y^{-1}\):

\[-\frac{du}{dx} + \frac{1}{x}\,u = x.\]

Multiply through by \(-1\) to obtain the standard linear form:

\[\frac{du}{dx} - \frac{1}{x}\,u = -x.\]

This is a linear ODE in \(u\) and can be solved with an integrating factor.

Page 11

Bernoulli ODEs — General Method

Definition: A Bernoulli equation has the form

\[\frac{dy}{dx} + P(x)\,y = Q(x)\,y^n, \quad n \geq 1 \text{ (integer)}.\]

Divide through by \(y^n\):

\[y^{-n}\frac{dy}{dx} + P(x)\,y^{1-n} = Q(x).\]

The substitution \(u = y^{1-n}\) gives:

\[\frac{du}{dx} = (1-n)\,y^{-n}\frac{dy}{dx}, \quad \text{i.e.,} \quad \frac{1}{1-n}\frac{du}{dx} = y^{-n}\frac{dy}{dx}.\]

Page 12

Bernoulli ODEs — Continued

Substituting \(u\) into the divided equation:

\[\frac{1}{1-n}\frac{du}{dx} + P(x)\,u = Q(x).\]

Multiply through by \((1-n)\):

\[\frac{du}{dx} + (1-n)P(x)\,u = (1-n)Q(x).\]

This is a linear ODE in \(u\). Use the integrating factor technique to solve for \(u = F(x)\).

Once \(u\) is found, solve for \(y\):

\[y = u^{\frac{1}{1-n}} = F(x)^{\frac{1}{1-n}}.\]

Page 13

Theoretical Considerations — Existence & Uniqueness

Observe that the ODE

\[\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{y}{x} + x\]

has general solution \(y = x^2 + Cx\). There are infinitely many solutions to the initial value problem

\[y(0) = 0.\]

(Any value of \(C\) satisfies \(0 = 0 + C \cdot 0 = 0\).) Uh-oh.

This shows that without additional conditions, an IVP may fail to have a unique solution. The issue here is that \(f(x,y) = \dfrac{y}{x} + x\) is not continuous at \(x = 0\).

Page 14

Theorem (Picard–Lindelöf)

Theorem (Picard–Lindelöf): If \(\dfrac{dy}{dx} = f(x,y)\) and \(f(x,y)\) is continuous on the rectangular domain

\[[a,b] \times [c,d],\]

then for each point \((x_0, y_0)\) in this region there exists a unique solution through \((x_0, y_0)\).

Diagram: A coordinate plane with horizontal axis labeled \(x\) and vertical axis labeled \(y\). A shaded rectangle is drawn with its left edge at \(x = a\), right edge at \(x = b\), bottom edge at \(y = c\), and top edge at \(y = d\), representing the domain of continuity of \(f\).

Page 15

Consequence of Picard–Lindelöf

Consequence: On the domain where \(f(x,y)\) is continuous, solution curves never intersect.

This follows directly from uniqueness: if two solution curves passed through the same point \((x_0, y_0)\), then by the Picard–Lindelöf theorem they would have to be the same solution, a contradiction.

Page 16

First Order ODE — Summary

The first-order portion of the course covers four major themes:

Modeling → Population/Logistic, Tanks/Zombie Gas, Circuit problems.

Visualizations → Draw and identify slope fields and solution curves.

Numerical → Compute and explain Euler’s Method.

Analytic → Identify and solve: separable, linear, exact; substitutions: homogeneous & Bernoulli.
